approx_date: Initial restatement announcement: ~May 2014 (Item 4.02 non-reliance filing); SEC settlement: December 2018
event_description: Yes. Hertz Global Holdings had a significant accounting restatement episode beginning in 2014. In May 2014, Hertz filed an Item 4.02 notice of non-reliance, announcing that its previously issued financial statements for fiscal years 2011, 2012, and part of 2013 (and related quarterly filings) should no longer be relied upon due to accounting errors. Issues included improper capitalization of certain non-fleet costs, errors in allowance for uncollectible accounts/receivables in Brazil, and other adjustments. This led to a lengthy delay in filing timely financial statements, an internal investigation by the Audit Committee, and significant management turnover, including the departure/resignation of CFO Elyse Douglas and later CEO Mark Frissora (late 2014), amid the fallout from the accounting problems (though Frissora's departure was tied to broader performance/governance issues as well as the accounting matter). The company subsequently restated its financials (filed delayed 10-Ks covering 2012-2014 together in 2015). The SEC opened an investigation into these accounting practices, which culminated in December 2018 when Hertz agreed to pay a $16 million civil penalty to settle SEC charges related to improper earnings management and accounting errors in the 2011-2013 period.
